retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Treatment / Management -- Storage Media
retrieved_docs.content: Storing the avulsed tooth in an isotonic solution, such as milk, saline, or saliva, slows down cell death in the periodontal ligament of the root. [10]  However, PDL cell death is unavoidable, and the storage in a solution is only a temporary but effective way of managing the tooth before replantation. Short-time storage in an isotonic solution has proven to provide the same or even higher healing results than replanting the tooth immediately. [11] [12] [11] Milk is the most frequently used and recommended storage solution due to its ready availability, appropriate pH, normal osmolarity, an abundance of nutrients, and growth factors. [9] It is worth noting that drinking water can damage the PDL due to its low osmolality.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Avulsions
retrieved_docs.content: Before replantation, it is recommended that the avulsed tooth be stored in an isotonic solution to prevent apoptosis of the periodontal ligament. Such acceptable solutions include milk, normal saline, or the patient’s saliva. Cell death of the periodontal ligament is inevitable and can only be temporarily delayed with a proper storage medium. [60] The alveolar bone should be gently irrigated with normal saline, and any obstructing blood clot should be aspirated before replantation.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An avulsion is a rare type of dental trauma, emphasizing the need for professionals to regularly review the treatment protocols to increase a successful outcome. Keeping in mind that the treatment aims to maintain the tooth and bone until complete facial growth is achieved. The need for early replantation and the appropriate storage medium is less recognized by patients, parents, and medical professionals. It is necessary for primary care providers, nurse practitioners, and the general dentist to educate the public regarding the prognosis and success of replantation, how early treatment is essential, and that the preferred storage medium is a balanced salt solution or milk.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A history of trauma to the tooth will reveal tooth avulsion, and the mechanism of injury can point to other injuries. History should include the length of time since tooth avulsion, the storage medium in which the tooth had been placed, and if the tooth is primary or permanent. By 14 years of age, a patient's primary teeth should all be replaced by permanent teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most common causes of dentoalveolar trauma include falls, especially in children, cycling, full-contact sports, traffic accidents, and assault. Dental trauma most frequently occurs at home, school, and sports centers.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Prognosis -- Tooth Avulsions
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for replanted tooth avulsions depends on several items, particularly the extra-alveolar period. While replantation should be attempted for all avulsed teeth, the outcomes are best for cases where replantation occurs within 30 minutes, the tooth is stored in an appropriate storage medium, and there are minimal concomitant dentoalveolar injuries. The success of replanted teeth markedly decreases once the tooth exceeds 60 minutes of extra-alveolar time. [69]

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the epidemiology of tooth avulsion. Review the treatment regime for an avulsed tooth. Explain the pathophysiology and histopathology of an avulsed tooth. Summarize the complications usually associated with tooth avulsion. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Avulsions
retrieved_docs.content: The tooth should be replanted in the correct anatomic alignment with firm pressure into the alveolar socket. It should then be stabilized with a passive and flexible splint. [9] Immobilization with a splint allows the periodontal ligament fibers to reform connections between the alveolus and the cementum. It is recommended that patients follow up to have their splint rechecked or removed at 2 weeks. If, at this time, the tooth is not viable, root canal therapy is indicated. If the tooth is viable, radiographs should be obtained at the 2-week, 1-month, 3-month, and 6-month follow-up appointments to evaluate for resorption of the tooth. Root canal therapy or even tooth extraction may be indicated if resorption develops. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Treatment / Management -- Follow up Appointment
retrieved_docs.content: The splint must be removed after two weeks, where the tooth will be clinically and radiographically examined. After removing the splint, the tooth’s mobility is checked, and a pulp vitality test should be performed, preferably pulse oximetry or electric testing. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Treatment / Management -- Anti-resorption Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: It involves soaking the avulsed tooth in a storage solution containing antibiotics. Anti-resorption therapy is believed to prevent inflammation caused by necrotic cells and microbial contamination. [9] Different regimes have been suggested; one includes a storage solution with 800 µg doxycycline and 640 µg dexamethasone for 20 minutes. [9] In the meantime, the alveolus should be lightly irrigated with 0.9% physiologic saline solution and aspirated gently if a blood clot is obstructing it.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Replantation
retrieved_docs.content: The tooth should be aligned anatomically and replanted, applying firm pressure to the socket. Most teeth can be successfully replanted if the extraoral dry time is less than 30 minutes; after this period, the survival probability of the tooth diminishes - the periodontal ligament cells are irreversibly damaged after 30 to 60 minutes.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Dislocation -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Before arrival in the hospital, there are no established universal protocols for prehospital care of mandibular dislocation. The basis for a transport decision is on patient stability and other factors that may have contributed to the injury, for example, trauma, shock, or altered mental status. So long as the patient has no airway symptoms, transport can proceed.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Dentoalveolar trauma injuries are caused by an external impact on the teeth, palate, mandible, maxilla, and gingiva. These injuries can result in anything from a tooth contusion to a total dislocation of the tooth from its socket, known as tooth avulsion. A significant amount of force is usually necessary for the avulsion of teeth, and other injuries to surrounding structures should be suspected. This activity reviews the evaluation, diagnosis, and management of tooth avulsion and highlights the role of team-based interprofessional care for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: A study published by Karayilmaz et al., examining the long-term prognosis of avulsed teeth, concluded that the replantation of avulsed teeth is a highly successful procedure. However, the long and short-term prognosis is strongly affected by the dry time. In another study, the long-term survival of replanted avulsed teeth was only 20%, which was attributed to the lack of follow-up treatment. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Treatment / Management -- Follow up Appointment
retrieved_docs.content: Immature teeth where the root has not yet wholly formed have a greater chance of revascularization after soaking in doxycycline. Primary teeth should not undergo replantation as this can damage the underlying permanent tooth germ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198
retrieved_docs.content: Nasogastric tube procedures classify the nasogastric tube, indicating that the range of clinical actions, techniques, and protocols are organized around the use, insertion, management, and care of the nasogastric tube.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 172
retrieved_docs.content: reconstructions—are organized under “other procedures associated with delivery.” Overall, the framework interrelates antenatal/postnatal care, multilingual terminology, instrumental deliveries (forceps, vacuum, breech extraction), fetal‑head manipulations (manual rotation, conversion, versions), induction methods (AROM, membrane sweeping, therapeutic agents), and tissue‑repair procedures, providing a structured model for comprehensive maternal‑fetal management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2909
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery by double application of forceps is a obstetric procedure in which the fetal head serves as the direct site of the intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 176
retrieved_docs.content: these interrelated entities illustrate a hierarchical, cross‑lingual organization of routine and assisted obstetric procedures, perineal‑trauma classifications, and maternal‑child care, providing clinicians with a comprehensive framework for managing labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery are classified as a subset of other procedures to assist delivery, indicating that these specific interventions are encompassed within the broader category of techniques used to facilitate and support the childbirth process.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2421
retrieved_docs.content: Antepartum Procedures is a medical category that includes, as its child, the subcategory Antepartum Procedures: Application, Insertion, Removal, indicating that the broader group of antepartum interventions is organized into specific actions of applying, inserting, and removing devices or treatments during the prenatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2791
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy and تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي (artificial membrane rupture) are both classified under الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد (obstetric therapeutic procedures). By recognizing these interventions as subcomponents of the broader category of obstetric therapeutic care, clinicians can better organize treatment options, understand their related indications, and apply appropriate management strategies within the field of obstetrics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2327
retrieved_docs.content: Application of therapeutic agent and delivery of vertex presentation are both classified as child procedures within the broader category of surgical manipulation, indicating that these specific interventions are considered subtypes of surgical manipulation in medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: related and possibly synonymous with “other procedures to assist delivery,” reinforcing its placement within this overarching group. Finally, “Medical and surgical induction of labor” is a child of “Medical or surgical induction of labor” and is primarily mapped from “artificial rupture of membranes to assist delivery,” illustrating that the combined medical‑surgical approach derives from the same membrane‑rupture technique. This integrated hierarchy clarifies how various cervical procedures, induction methods, and membrane‑manipulation techniques interrelate within the context of labor induction and assisted delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2912
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures is a broad category of obstetric interventions that encompasses specific techniques such as بضع الفرج and Vaginal Assisted Delivery, each classified as a child procedure within this group. Both بضع الفرج and Vaginal Assisted Delivery represent assisted vaginal delivery methods used to facilitate safe childbirth when spontaneous vaginal delivery is not optimal, highlighting the importance of tailored procedural options in maternal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2393
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is a fetal procedure that is classified among other delivery procedures, indicating that it is a type of intervention performed on the fetus to reposition it prior to birth and is grouped within the broader category of delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 206
retrieved_docs.content: “Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery” also includes the concrete example فشل التوليد بالملقط (failed delivery with forceps). Collectively, this hierarchical and functional network links broad procedural categories (ante‑partum, intra‑partum, surgical manipulation, cervical incision, induction, pregnancy‑related care) to their specific clinical actions (amniocentesis, chorionic villus sampling, fetal reduction, various breech and forceps deliveries, obstetrical version techniques, membrane rupture, cervical dilation, trachelotomy, evacuation of retained products, ectopic‑pregnancy surgery, episiotomy repair). Understanding these interrelationships supports clinicians in selecting appropriate obstetric interventions, clarifying synonymous terminology across languages, and optimizing patient care throughout pregnancy management and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetric Procedure is defined as a hierarchical class that includes ante‑partum, intrapartum, and post‑partum interventions. Child categories comprise Antepartum Procedures (application, insertion, removal) such as fetal reduction, amniocentesis, placental biopsy, amnioinfusion, uterine curettage, and other placental surgeries; Intrapartum procedures such as vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy or forceps), perineal incision, manual fetal rotation, intrapartum fetal stimulation, sweeping of membranes, artificial rupture of membranes (ARM) and its surgical subtype, cervical dilation/incision, obstetrical trachelotomy, force‑force delivery, removal of retained pregnancy products, and cardiac puncture; and Post‑partum obstetric operations including repair of obstetric laceration, severe delivery procedures, and immediate post‑delivery care. Parent classes include Female Genital and Obstetric Procedures, Other Procedures Inducing or Assisting Delivery, and Other Procedures to

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2135
retrieved_docs.content: Sweeping of membrane is directly related to the procedure known as artificial rupture of membranes to assist delivery, a technique used to promote labor. The artificial rupture of membranes to assist delivery is performed on the amnion, making the amnion the specific anatomical site where this intervention is applied.
